perturbation.original_text: 1.2. “ Confidential Information ” – Confidential Information is any information of the Discloser of actual or potential commercial value owing to the fact that it is unknown to t hird parties; disclosed by the Discloser to the Recipient in writing, by way of document delivery, including in an electronic format safeguarded from unauthorised access to the information in transit; related to business or financial plans or strategies, f inancial reports or accounting (except to the extent contemplated by the law), contractual relations, pricing and marketing of goods, products or services, technical information, commercial secrets, know -how, research, production plans, concepts, intellect ual property items (including inventions, discoveries, innovative ideas, useful models, designs, production prototypes not covered by patent for whatever reason, computer programmes, databases, trademark designs not registered for whatever reason). No info rmation which has become public and to which the Discloser has provided access for third parties without limitation or which has become public other than through the fault of the Recipient (but not before it becomes publicly circulated), which the Recipien t is able to prove that it had been in the possession of before it was disclosed by the Discloser or that such information was provided without liability to maintain confidentiality, will be deemed confidential. No information which the Recipient is able t o prove it has created without using Confidential Information will be deemed confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2. “ Confidential Information ” – Confidential Information is any information disclosed by the Discloser to the Recipient; related to business strategies, financial reports, contractual relations, pricing and marketing, technical information, commercial secrets, know -how, research, production plans, concepts, intellectual property items, computer programmes, databases, trademark designs. No information which the Recipient is able t o prove it has created without using Confidential Information will be deemed confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'of actual or potential commercial value owing to the fact that it is unknown to t hird parties' and 'in writing, by way of document delivery, including in an electronic format safeguarded from unauthorised access to the information in transit' and 'No info rmation which has become public and to which the Discloser has provided access for third parties without limitation or which has become public other than through the fault of the Recipient (but not before it becomes publicly circulated), which the Recipien t is able to prove that it had been in the possession of before it was disclosed by the Discloser or that such information was provided without liability to maintain confidentiality, will be deemed confidential.' This creates an ambiguity, making the definition of Confidential Information overly broad and potentially including information that should not be considered confidential, thus weakening the protection afforded to the Discloser, contradicting section 4. Exceptions. This Agreement imposes no obligation on a Recipient with respect to Confidential Informat ion that: (a) is or becomes publicly known through no breach of this Agreement
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Exceptions. This Agreement imposes no obligation on a Recipient with respect to Confidential Informat ion that: (a) is or becomes publicly known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is rightfully received by the Recipient from a third party that has no applicable duty of confidentiality; (c) is or becomes the subject of a patent, in which case a party shall rely upon its rights under applicable patent law; (d) is disclosed by the Recipient with the prior written consent of the Discloser. A party also may disclose Confidential Information if required by court order, governmental demand, or other co mpulsory legal process, provided that if legally permitted to do so it first notify the Discloser in writing at least ten (10) days in advance in order to afford Discloser an opportunity to seek a protective order or other relief.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing key qualifiers such as 'of actual or potential commercial value owing to the fact that it is unknown to third parties' and 'in writing, by way of document delivery, including in an electronic format safeguarded from unauthorised access to the information in transit'. This creates a direct contradiction with Section 4, 'Exceptions', which states that no obligation is imposed on the recipient with respect to Confidential Information that 'is or becomes publicly known through no breach of this Agreement'. By broadening the definition of Confidential Information, the changed text includes information that would previously have been excluded under the original definition and therefore subject to the exception in Section 4. This makes Section 4 unclear and potentially unenforceable, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3. Neither Party will disclose the fact of existence of this Agreement without prior approval from t he other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3. Either Party may disclose the existence of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Neither Party will disclose the fact of existence of this Agreement without prior approval from the other Party' to 'Either Party may disclose the existence of this Agreement' contradicts with section 2.4. The Recipient will ensure that information is stored securely and cannot be accessed by any individuals, except the Representatives, including outside business hours.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.4. The Recipient will ensure that information is stored securely and cannot be accessed by any individuals, except the Representatives, including outside business hours.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that neither party could disclose the agreement without approval. The changed text allows either party to disclose the agreement. This directly contradicts section 2.4, which requires the recipient to keep information secure. If either party can disclose the existence of the agreement, this undermines the requirement for secure information storage, creating a contradiction. The explanation and the definition of 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' are adhered to. The change removes a restriction (non-disclosure without approval) which creates a contradiction with obligations stated in another section (secure information storage).

perturbation.original_text: 3.  Permitted Use. Recipient shall keep Discloser’s Confidentia l Information confidential and use Confidential Information only for the above -stated Purpose of Disclosure, or for performing its obligations or exercising its rights under this Agreement. Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information internally wit hin its organization (including Affiliates) only on a “need -to-know” basis, and except for Cardholder Information which shall not be disclosed, to its outside attorneys, consultants, contractors and sub -contractors (“ Representatives ”), only to the extent t hose individuals are bound by a legal obligation or agreement that is as protective of the Confidential Information as 2 this Agreement. A Recipient is fully responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Affiliates or Representative s. A Recipient shall use the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as it uses to prevent unauthorized disclosure of its own Confidential Information. Except for the permitted uses and disclosures expressly set out in this Agree ment, no other uses or disclosures are permitted.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Permitted Use. Recipient shall use Confidential Information for the above -stated Purpose of Disclosure. Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information internally wit hin its organization (including Affiliates). A Recipient is fully responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Affiliates or Representative s.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'Recipient shall keep Discloser’s Confidentia l Information confidential' and 'only to the extent t hose individuals are bound by a legal obligation or agreement that is as protective of the Confidential Information as 2 this Agreement.' and 'A Recipient shall use the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as it uses to prevent unauthorized disclosure of its own Confidential Information. Except for the permitted uses and disclosures expressly set out in this Agree ment, no other uses or disclosures are permitted.' weakens the confidentiality obligations. Now the Recipient is no longer explicitly required to keep the information confidential, contradicting section 10. Liability of the parties. A Party defaulting under this Agreement will reimburse the other Party for any loss resulting from disclosure or unlawful use of Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. Liability of the parties. A Party defaulting under this Agreement will reimburse the other Party for any loss resulting from disclosure or unlawful use of Confidential Information. Loss es will be reimbursed according to the law of Singapore .
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit requirement for the recipient to keep the Discloser's Confidential Information confidential, removes the requirement of a legal obligation or agreement to protect the Confidential Information, and removes the reasonable degree of care required to prevent unauthorized disclosure. This directly contradicts section 10, which states that a party defaulting under the agreement will reimburse the other party for any loss resulting from disclosure or unlawful use of Confidential Information. Without the obligation to keep the information confidential, the liability clause in section 10 becomes difficult to enforce, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Mandatory Disclosure. If the Recipient is required by the law to disclose Confidential Information, it will advise the Discloser immediately. The Recipient undertakes to disclose information exclusively to the extent required by the applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Mandatory Disclosure. If the Recipient is required by the law to disclose Confidential Information, it will advise the Discloser when convenient.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'immediately' to 'when convenient' reduces the obligation to provide timely notice of legally compelled disclosure. This creates an in-text contradiction with the Discloser's rights mentioned in section 6. Ownership. As between th e parties, Discloser and Recipient, respectively, will retain all right, title and interest to any Confidential Information provided or disclosed by such party under this Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Ownership. As between th e parties, Discloser and Recipient, respectively, will retain all right, title and interest to any Confidential Information provided or disclosed by such party under this Agreement. The Discloser reserves the right to analyze the actions taken by the Recip ient to safeguard Confidential Information. If the Recipient refuses to share information on the actions it is taking to safeguard Confidential Information or if such actions are found to be inadequate for safeguarding Confidential Information, the Disclos er may refuse to disclose Confidential Information or unilaterally refuse to perform under any agreement made between the Parties, by which the Recipient is to receive or has received access to Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 5 by replacing 'immediately' with 'when convenient' regarding the Recipient's obligation to notify the Discloser of legally compelled disclosure. This creates a contradiction with Section 6, which reserves the Discloser's right to analyze the Recipient's actions to safeguard Confidential Information and potentially refuse to disclose further information if those actions are inadequate. The delay introduced by 'when convenient' impairs the Discloser's ability to promptly assess and react to potential breaches, directly contradicting the Discloser's rights and powers outlined in Section 6 to safeguard confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Return or Destruction. Upon Disc loser’s written request, a Recipient shall promptly return all Confidential Information received under this Agreement, together with all copies, or certify in writing that it has destroyed all such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Return or Destruction. Upon Discloser’s request, a Recipient will consider returning Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation for prompt return or destruction of Confidential Information upon request and replacing it with 'will consider' contradicts Section 11. Remedies. The parties acknowledge that improper disclosure of Confidential Information may cause irreparable injury to the Discloser, and that remedies at law for any such breach could be inadeq uate.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. Remedies. The parties acknowledge that improper disclosure of Confidential Information may cause irreparable injury to the Discloser, and that remedies at law for any such breach could be inadeq uate. In the event of a breach or threatened breach, Discloser has the right to seek injunctive relief (in addition to any and all other remedies available at law or equity) without the need to post a bond or other security or demonstrate the confidential nature of its Confidential Information. The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement is entitled to its reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees. Each party irrevocably waives any right it may have to a trial by jury in connection with any dispute arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that upon the Discloser's written request, the Recipient 'shall promptly return all Confidential Information received...or certify in writing that it has destroyed all such materials.' The changed text modifies this obligation to 'will consider returning Confidential Information.' This change from a mandatory action (return/destruction) to a discretionary consideration directly contradicts Section 11, which provides remedies for 'improper disclosure of Confidential Information,' implying that the Discloser has a right to prevent such disclosure via the return/destruction of said information.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Duration of Obligations. Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end five (5) years after the date of first disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Duration of Obligations. Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement are indefinite.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end five (5) years after the date of first disclosure hereunder' to 'Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement are indefinite.' this clause now contradicts section 8. No Commitment. Each party acknowledges that the other party may be evaluating similar business opportunities or proposals with third parties, or developing systems, products, or services that are similar in nature to the other party’s current or planned offerings.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. No Commitment. Each party acknowledges that the other party may be evaluating similar business opportunities or proposals with third parties, or developing systems, products, or services that are similar in nature to the other party’s current or planned offerings. Nothi ng in this Agreement limits the right of either party to develop, procure or market systems, products, or services that may be similar to or competitive with those of the other party; provided that no unauthorized use or disclosure of the other party’s Con fidential Information is used in such activities. In addition, nothing in this Agreement will be construed to prevent either party from entering into negotiations or business relationships with any third party, even if such third party is a competitor of a party to this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a duration of five years for the Recipient's obligations. The changed text makes these obligations indefinite. This directly contradicts section 8, which states that each party may evaluate similar business opportunities or proposals with third parties. If the Recipient's obligations are indefinite, it would severely limit their ability to engage in competitive activities, as permitted by section 8. Therefore, a clear and direct contradiction exists.
